title: Create /os/[concept] static routes with server-rendered educational content
description: OS module is invisible to search engines — all content at client-rendered '/' with no dedicated URLs. Create 6 static routes: /os/cpu-scheduling, /os/page-replacement, /os/deadlock, /os/memory, /os/mem-alloc, /os/thread-sync. Each page SSR's concept explanation + embeds interactive canvas client-side. Pattern: mirror /concepts/[slug]/page.tsx which uses generateStaticParams + generateMetadata + JSON-LD. Source: SEO-001 finding.
acceptanceCriteria: 6 routes exist under /os/[concept] with generateStaticParams
Each page has server-rendered concept explanation (2-3 paragraphs)
Interactive canvas loaded client-side with dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })
Crawlers receive meaningful HTML content for each concept
URL structure: /os/cpu-scheduling (not /os?concept=cpu-scheduling)

title: Add generateMetadata + OG images + JSON-LD to OS concept pages
description: Once /os/[concept] routes exist, add: generateMetadata() with concept-specific title/description, OG image via existing /api/og endpoint (supports title+type+difficulty params), generateLearningResourceJsonLd and generateBreadcrumbJsonLd from existing src/lib/seo/json-ld.ts. Source: SEO-002, SEO-005.
acceptanceCriteria: Each OS concept page has unique <title> and <meta description>
OG image generated via /api/og?title=CPU+Scheduling&type=os-concept&difficulty=Beginner
LearningResource JSON-LD schema on each page
BreadcrumbList JSON-LD: Home > OS Concepts > CPU Scheduling
Twitter card meta tags included

title: Add OS concept slugs to sitemap.ts
description: sitemap.ts at src/app/sitemap.ts has entries for /problems/, /blog/, /concepts/, /lld-problems/, /patterns/, /interviews/ — but zero OS entries. Add 6 OS concept URLs with priority 0.8 and changeFrequency 'monthly'. Source: SEO-004.
acceptanceCriteria: sitemap.ts includes /os/cpu-scheduling through /os/thread-sync
Priority: 0.8 (same as concepts)
Change frequency: monthly
Sitemap validates with Google Search Console

title: Instrument OS module with analytics — concept views, simulation runs, comparisons
description: OSModule has ZERO analytics events. The useAnalytics() hook and PostHog infrastructure exist but are unused. Add: os_concept_viewed when setActive is called, os_simulation_run on all 7 run callbacks, os_compare_all_used on comparison callbacks, os_belady_anomaly_viewed on Belady demo, os_steps_completed when user reaches last step. Source: ANA-001, ANA-004.
acceptanceCriteria: os_concept_viewed fires on every concept switch (line 1982 setActive call)
os_simulation_run fires on all run callbacks with concept + algorithm metadata
os_compare_all_used fires on comparison callbacks
os_belady_anomaly_viewed fires on Belady demo
os_steps_completed fires when user clicks 'Show All' or reaches last step
All events include concept type and algorithm name as properties
Events no-op gracefully when PostHog key is not configured

title: Add OS-specific event names to analytics event catalog
description: analytics.ts and posthog.ts event catalogs have generic events but no OS-specific ones. Add: OS_CONCEPT_VIEWED, OS_SIMULATION_RUN, OS_COMPARE_ALL_USED, OS_BELADY_VIEWED, OS_STEPS_COMPLETED, OS_AUTOPLAY_USED, OS_INVALID_INPUT to both catalogs with proper typing. Source: ANA-003.
acceptanceCriteria: AnalyticsEvent enum includes all 7 OS events
PostHogEvent enum mirrors with same events
Type-safe event properties defined for each event
Console.log in dev mode shows event name + properties

title: Wire OS module strings through i18n strings.ts — concept names, algorithm names, UI labels
description: strings.ts has modules.os and modules.osDesc but OSModule.tsx doesn't import it. ~200+ English strings are hardcoded. Phase 1: Add os.* section to strings.ts covering concept names (6), algorithm names (14), button labels (~15), section headers (~10), empty state messages (~6). Phase 2: Import and use in OSSidebar, concept arrays, and button labels. Source: I18N-001, I18N-002.
acceptanceCriteria: strings.ts has os.concepts.cpuScheduling.name, os.concepts.cpuScheduling.description, etc.
strings.ts has os.algorithms.fcfs, os.algorithms.sjf, etc.
strings.ts has os.ui.run, os.ui.compareAll, os.ui.addProcess, etc.
OSSidebar reads from strings.os.concepts
SCHEDULING_ALGOS reads from strings.os.algorithms
Button labels read from strings.os.ui

title: Replace .toFixed() with locale-aware number formatting in OS metrics
description: ~30 instances of .toFixed(n) in OSModule.tsx produce '2.67' instead of '2,67' for German/French locales. Create a formatNumber(val, decimals) utility and replace all .toFixed() calls in metrics displays (avgWait, TAT, hitRate, cpuUtilization, fragmentation, P50/P99). Source: I18N-004.
acceptanceCriteria: formatNumber utility in src/lib/i18n/format.ts
All .toFixed() in OSModule.tsx replaced with formatNumber()
German locale shows '2,67' instead of '2.67'
Percentage formatting consistent (e.g., '95,5%' in German)

title: Refactor engine event descriptions from baked strings to {type, args} pattern for i18n
description: All engine files (scheduling.ts, page-replacement.ts, deadlock.ts, etc.) have ~100+ template literal description strings baked into the engine layer. These are displayed directly in the Event Log. For i18n, engines should return structured {messageKey, args} objects, and a separate formatter generates the localized string. This is a significant refactor but is the only correct approach for translation. Source: I18N-003.
acceptanceCriteria: Engine events return { messageKey: 'os.scheduling.processStarts', args: { name: 'P1', burst: 5 } }
A formatEventDescription(event, locale) function generates the display string
English formatter produces identical output to current template literals
Event Log component calls formatter instead of reading description directly
No change in visual output for English locale

title: Add Priority Inversion visualization with Mars Pathfinder case study
description: Priority inversion is one of the most famous real-world OS bugs (Mars Pathfinder 1997). Three tasks (high/medium/low priority): low holds mutex needed by high, medium preempts low, high starves. Then show priority inheritance fix. Highly visualizable as a Gantt timeline. Source: Cornell CS4410, Chalmers, curriculum agent P0 finding.
acceptanceCriteria: 3-task simulation: high, medium, low priority with shared mutex
Gantt chart showing the inversion: high blocked, medium running, low holding mutex
Priority inheritance fix mode: low boosted to high's priority while holding mutex
Mars Pathfinder story in Learn tab: 'This bug nearly ended a $265M NASA mission'
Step descriptions explain WHY high is blocked despite being highest priority

title: Add Copy-on-Write (COW) fork visualization
description: COW fork is a top-10 OS interview question ('How does fork() work?'). After fork, parent and child share physical pages. On write, a page fault triggers a copy. Show: shared page table entries with reference counts, write triggers copy, reference count decrement. Source: MIT 6.1810 COW lab, curriculum agent P0.
acceptanceCriteria: Dual address space view: parent and child processes
Shared pages shown as single physical frame with arrows from both page tables
Reference count visible on each physical frame
Write to shared page triggers: page fault → copy → separate frames
Reference count decrements correctly
Real-world note: 'Redis uses COW fork for background persistence (RDB snapshots)'

title: Add Buffer Overflow + Stack Smashing visualization for security concepts
description: Buffer overflow is the single most impactful security visualization. Show: stack frame with local buffer, saved frame pointer, return address. User 'types' input; as it exceeds buffer bounds, it visually overwrites return address. Then show defenses: stack canaries, ASLR, DEP/NX bit. Source: curriculum agent P0, Montana State CS, RIT.
acceptanceCriteria: Interactive stack frame diagram showing buffer, SFP, return address
User input field that visually 'fills' the buffer
Overflow visually corrupts return address (color change to red)
Defense modes: stack canary (detects overflow), ASLR (randomizes addresses)
Step-through with explanation of each phase
Real-world: 'Morris Worm (1988) used buffer overflow. Still the #1 vulnerability class.'

title: Add Sleeping Barber Problem to sync visualizations
description: Classic sync problem: barber sleeps when no customers, wakes when customer arrives, waiting room with N chairs. Tests semaphore and mutex usage. Excellent visual metaphor (barber shop). Source: Tanenbaum, Silberschatz, curriculum agent P0.
acceptanceCriteria: Animated barber shop: barber chair, waiting room with N chairs, door
Customer arrival/departure animation
Semaphore counters visible (waiting customers, barber state)
Edge case: full waiting room → customer leaves
Step descriptions explain synchronization logic

title: Add H2O Water Molecule Building Problem (LeetCode 1117)
description: H2O is a LeetCode Concurrency problem testing barrier/semaphore knowledge. Hydrogen and oxygen atoms arrive; group into 2H+1O molecules. Excellent chemistry metaphor. High interview frequency for concurrency roles. Source: LeetCode 1117, Virginia Tech CS3214, curriculum agent P1.
acceptanceCriteria: Animated atoms: H (blue circles) and O (red circles) arriving from queue
Waiting area accumulates atoms
When 2H + 1O ready: bonding animation forming water molecule
Barrier/semaphore counters visible
LeetCode reference: 'This is LeetCode 1117 — commonly asked at Google, Amazon'

title: Add DMA (Direct Memory Access) visualization
description: DMA is a fundamental I/O concept: CPU initiates transfer, DMA controller moves data while CPU does other work, interrupt on completion. Compare with programmed I/O (CPU busy-waiting). Source: all OS textbooks, curriculum agent P1.
acceptanceCriteria: Three-entity diagram: CPU, DMA Controller, Memory
Animation: CPU configures DMA → DMA transfers blocks → CPU works on other tasks → interrupt
Comparison mode: programmed I/O (CPU busy-waiting) vs DMA (CPU free)
Step descriptions explain each phase
Metric: CPU utilization comparison between PIO and DMA

title: Add ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) visualization
description: Side-by-side comparison of fixed vs randomized address space layouts. Show how ASLR prevents return-to-libc attacks by randomizing stack, heap, and library positions. Low effort, high visual impact. Source: curriculum agent P1.
acceptanceCriteria: Two process address space diagrams: fixed layout vs randomized
Each run randomizes positions differently
Attacker arrow trying to jump to fixed address: succeeds in fixed layout, fails in randomized
Explanation: 'ASLR randomizes stack, heap, and shared library addresses on every execution'
Real-world: 'Enabled by default in Linux since 2.6.12 (2005), Windows since Vista'

title: Add Interrupt Handling visualization — CPU timeline with ISR execution
description: Interrupt handling is a week 1-2 OS topic. Show: CPU executing instructions → interrupt signal → context save → ISR execution → context restore → resume. Include interrupt vector table lookup. Source: all OS textbooks, curriculum agent P1.
acceptanceCriteria: CPU execution timeline with running instructions
Interrupt signal as vertical arrow disrupting timeline
Context save animation (registers pushed to stack)
ISR highlighted in different color
Context restore and resume
Interrupt vector table shown as lookup step

title: Add aria-live='polite' to all 6 concept result containers
description: WCAG critical failure: when user clicks Run, results appear silently — screen readers announce nothing. Zero aria-live regions exist in 3933-line file. Wrap each result display region in <div aria-live='polite' aria-atomic='true'>. Affects scheduling Gantt (line 2465), page grid (line 2583), deadlock RAG (line 2809), memory translation (line 2978), alloc bar (line 3261), sync canvas (line 3665). Source: screen reader audit TASK-A5-01.
acceptanceCriteria: Each concept result area wrapped in aria-live='polite'
After clicking Run, screen reader announces 'Results available' or result summary
Step changes announced for step-through concepts
Comparison results announced when Compare All completes

title: Fix SVG legend text contrast — gray-500 fails AA on dark background
description: SVG legend text uses fill='#6b7280' (gray-500) on dark backgrounds. Measured contrast: ~3.5:1, fails WCAG AA requirement of 4.5:1 for normal text. Change to fill='#9ca3af' (gray-400, ~5.7:1) or use CSS variable var(--foreground-muted). Affects RAG legend (lines 1090-1094) and ThreadSync legend (lines 1322-1326). Source: screen reader audit TASK-A5-06.
acceptanceCriteria: All SVG text elements meet 4.5:1 contrast ratio on dark theme
Legend text in RAG and ThreadSync updated to gray-400 or higher
SVG process node text (blue-500 at 11px) also passes AA
Verified with contrast checker tool

title: Honor prefers-reduced-motion in GCPauseLatencyVisualizer
description: GCPauseLatencyVisualizer uses motion.div with animate={{ height: ... }} creating 10 new animated elements/second. Does NOT check prefers-reduced-motion despite the project having useReducedMotion() at src/providers/ReducedMotionProvider.tsx. Also animates width on heap bars, violating the project's own performanceLimits.neverAnimateProperties at src/lib/constants/motion.ts:803. Source: screen reader audit TASK-A5-05.
acceptanceCriteria: Import useReducedMotion from ReducedMotionProvider
When reduced motion preferred: disable animate props on timeline bars and heap bars
Use CSS transitions gated by prefers-reduced-motion media query instead
Stop animating width property on heap bars (use opacity or transform instead)

title: Add role='tab'/role='tabpanel' to bottom panel tabs (Event Log / Learn)
description: Bottom panel 'Event Log' and 'Learn' buttons are plain <button> elements without tab semantics. Screen readers don't know these are tabs. Add role='tablist' to container, role='tab' to each button, role='tabpanel' to content areas, aria-selected to active tab. Source: screen reader audit TASK-A5-08.
acceptanceCriteria: Tab container has role='tablist'
Each tab button has role='tab' and aria-selected
Content areas have role='tabpanel' and aria-labelledby pointing to tab
Arrow keys navigate between tabs (standard ARIA tab pattern)
Only active tabpanel is visible to screen reader

title: Add virtual scrolling to event log bottom panel for long simulations
description: Event log renders ALL events as real DOM nodes with no windowing. A 200-event simulation creates 200+ DOM nodes in the log. Thread sync with 8 threads can produce 100+ events. With MLFQ + 10 processes: 300+ events. Add react-window or similar virtualized list. Source: perf audit finding.
acceptanceCriteria: Event log uses virtualized list rendering (react-window or equivalent)
Only visible events are in the DOM (typically 15-20 rows)
Scroll behavior is smooth and native-feeling
Performance: no jank at 500+ events

title: Remove unused mlfq import and add barrel import tree-shake verification
description: OSModule.tsx:22 imports mlfq from @/lib/os but never calls it (mlfqScheduler from mlfq-scheduler.ts is used instead). Also, the barrel import @/lib/os re-exports all 8 engine modules — if tree-shaking fails, all ~80KB of engine code loads regardless of which concept is active. Remove unused import and verify tree-shaking with ANALYZE=true. Source: perf audit.
acceptanceCriteria: mlfq removed from import statement at OSModule.tsx:22
ANALYZE=true build run to verify barrel import tree-shakes correctly
Add 'analyze' script to package.json: 'ANALYZE=true next build'
If barrel defeats tree-shaking: switch to direct file imports
